text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic total proctocolectomy with intracorporeal ileal pouch anal anastomosis. Trochars are placed with the camera in the umbilicus and four additional trochars, allowing access to all quadrants of the colon. The surgeon stands on the right side of the patient and incises the gastrocolic omentum from the middle to the inferior pole of the spleen. The anterior layer of the mesocolon is incised at the inferior border of the pancreas, continuing the division of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon incises the posterior layer of the mesocolon at the duodenal jugular flexure, performing an en bloc dissection that includes the splenic flexure and mesocolon, separating them from the serratus fascia. The genitous fascia and pancreas are visualized. The surgeon then moves to the right side, standing on the left of the patient, and incises the gastrocolic ligament, identifying the second part of the duodenum. The hepatocolic ligament peritoneum is incised, and the geratose fascia is carefully identified as the second and third parts of the duodenum are dissected.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileocecal region by incising the posterior inferior border of the mesentery, creating a tunnel for retropaternal dissection. The right side of the mesocolon is dissected up to the duodenum and inferior vena cava. The ileum and colon are repositioned in their normal anatomical locations. The anterior layer of the mesentery is incised along the border of the supramesenteric vein. The ileocolic vessels are divided, starting with the vein and then the artery. The middle colic vessels are divided at the root of the mesentery near the inferior border of the pancreas, which is now completely free. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon are released, allowing complete mobilization of the right side of the colon, including the transverse colon up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon by dividing the inferior mesenteric vessels and dissecting the mesentery of the left colon from the posterior layer, ensuring the ureter and gonadal vessels remain undisturbed. Blunt dissection is used to clear the Gerota's fascia from the mesocolon layer, extending laterally to the left abdominal wall and cranially to the previously dissected area. The lateral border of the peritoneum is incised.

The surgeon continues the lateral peritoneal incision upwards along the colon, freeing the descending colon and rectosigmoid. Cranial traction is applied to stretch and incise the lateral peritoneal flap. Dissection proceeds through areolar tissues while protecting the nerves, which are clearly visible and intact. The mesorectum's anterior fascia remains intact without any bridging.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection near the liver, with the liver visibly exposed. The rectum is dissected up to the levator ani, and the surgeon applies pressure on the perineum to facilitate division at the levator ani level. The rectum is completely removed, revealing the staple line. The posterior vaginal wall is identified and divided. A cone is used to introduce a valve system with a grasper.

A plastic sheath is inserted through the cone, extending from the peritoneal cavity to the outside. The surgeon introduces the divided end of the bowel through the open plastic sheath and extracts the entire specimen, including the rectum, sigmoid colon, and part of the ileum, through the posterior vaginal wall. The distal ileum is folded to create a pouch, measured at fifteen centimeters using a tape. Two loops are joined with interrupted stitches, and the apex of the loop is divided with a stapler. Careful manipulation is required to position each jaw of the stapler into the bowel, allowing for the use of multiple cartridges to lengthen the pouch. The six-centimeter echelon stapler is used to extend the pouch size without difficulty.

The surgeon adjusts the bowel over the stapler using stitches, then inserts the anvil into the pouch. A proline suture is used to tie around the anvil, and the anvil shaft is held with a special instrument. A circular stapler is introduced through the anus, and the bowel ends are stapled together to create a circular anastomosis. The bowels are checked for proper rotation.

The surgeon removes the staple and inflates the bowel to check the integrity of the anastomosis, ensuring there is no leakage. The alignment of the vessels is verified for tension and rotation. A loop of small bowel proximal to the pouch is identified and used to create a split ileostomy on the right side of the abdominal wall. The specimen is shown.
